children.name: Glareola
children.description: Pratincoles — elegant, tern-like waders with long pointed wings and a forked tail. They catch insects on the wing like swallows, flying with a buoyant, swooping action. Their short legs reflect their aerial lifestyle. Nests on open ground in dry plains and salt pans.

children.name: Glareola pratincola
children.description: A graceful, tern-like wader of southern Europe, Africa, and Asia. Brown above, white belly, and a distinctive cream-coloured throat bordered by a black collar. In flight, the white rump and forked tail are conspicuous. Nests in colonies on dry open ground near water.

children.name: Glareola nordmanni
children.description: Very similar to the Collared Pratincole but with black underwings (not chestnut) and a shorter tail. Breeds in the steppes of Ukraine, Russia, and Kazakhstan. Winters in southern Africa. Classified as Near Threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Glareola maldivarum
children.description: The Asian pratincole, breeding from India through Southeast Asia to China and wintering in Indonesia and Australia. Olive-brown above, chestnut underwing. Migratory flocks often gather in huge numbers at traditional stopover sites.

children.name: Glareola ocularis
children.description: Endemic to Madagascar, where it breeds on inland lakes and rivers, migrating to East African coasts after breeding. Slaty-grey above, white below, with a distinctive white eye-stripe and red bill base.

children.name: Glareola lactea
children.description: The smallest pratincole, found on large rivers and lakeshores from the Indian subcontinent to Southeast Asia. It has pale sandy-brown upperparts, a white belly, and a short forked tail. It nests in loose colonies on sandbanks and is often seen hawking for insects at dusk.

children.name: Glareola nuchalis
children.description: A distinctive pratincole with a white collar on the hindneck, found along rocky rivers and rapids in sub-Saharan Africa. It has dark grey upperparts, a white belly, and a red base to the bill. It perches on rocks in mid-river, sallying out to catch insects over the water.

children.name: Glareola cinerea
children.description: A pale grey pratincole found along large rivers in West and Central Africa. It has grey upperparts, white underparts, and a distinctive white tail with a black terminal band. It perches on sandbanks and rocks in rivers, hawking for insects over the water surface.

children.name: Cursorius
children.description: Coursers — fast-running waders of arid plains, named for their speed across the ground. Unlike pratincoles, they rarely fly and prefer to run from danger, relying on their superb camouflage. Their elongated hind claws provide stability on loose sand. Found across Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia.

children.name: Cursorius cursor
children.description: A pale, sandy-coloured wader of the Sahara, Middle East, and the Canary Islands. Cream-buff plumage perfectly matches desert sand. It runs rapidly on long legs, stopping to pick insects from the ground. A rare but regular vagrant to southern Europe.

children.name: Cursorius temminckii
children.description: Found across sub-Saharan Africa in dry savanna and open woodland. Warm brown above, paler below, with a distinctive white supercilium and a chestnut nape patch. Named after the Dutch ornithologist Coenraad Jacob Temminck.

children.name: Cursorius coromandelicus
children.description: A chestnut-crowned courser of dry scrub and semi-desert across the Indian subcontinent. It has rufous upperparts, a white belly, and a black patch on the belly. It runs swiftly across dry plains, pausing to pick insects from the ground. Its call is a loud, piping whistle.

children.name: Cursorius rufus
children.description: A warm rufous-brown courser found in the arid interior of southern Africa. It has a distinctive white supercilium, a chestnut crown, and a white belly. Inhabits dry savanna and scrub, running swiftly on long legs. Named after the English naturalist William Burchell.

children.name: Rhinoptilus
children.description: Coursers with more heavily patterned plumage than Cursorius, often with bold facial markings and striking wing patterns. They are named for their distinctive nostril structure and are found in the drier regions of Africa and South Asia.

children.name: Rhinoptilus chalcopterus
children.description: A large, striking courser with a distinctive bronze-green iridescent wing patch, found across sub-Saharan Africa in dry savanna and woodland. It has a brown-and-white striped face, a white throat bordered by a black line, and a yellow bill with a black tip. It is crepuscular and often seen on roads at night.

children.name: Rhinoptilus cinctus
children.description: A handsome courser with three distinctive breast bands - black, white, and chestnut - found in dry savanna and scrub of eastern and southern Africa. It has a brown crown, grey-brown upperparts, and a white supercilium. It runs swiftly on long legs and is more crepuscular than other coursers.

children.name: Rhinoptilus africanus
children.description: A small, pale courser with two black breast bands and a sandy-brown back, found in dry stony plains and semi-deserts of eastern and southern Africa. It relies on its cryptic plumage for camouflage, freezing when approached. It runs rapidly with short bursts of speed.
